verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: But, I undertook to shew something of more importance still in what follows.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: After he had said that we had hoped in Christ before, and that in the time before the foundation of the world and before we were born in our bodies, we had been blessed and chosen in heaven, he again introduces that ‘other’ of his, and says: “Another, who does not admit this doctrine that we had a previous existence and had hope in Christ before we lived in this body, would have us understand the matter in his own way.”

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: In this passage this ‘other,’ whoever he may be, has put forth all his ill savour.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Let him tell us then whom he means by this ‘other’ who does not admit this opinion that before we lived in this body we both existed and hoped in Christ—for which he requires us to condemn Origen.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Whom does he wish us to understand by this ‘other’?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Is it some one opposed to himself?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: What do you say, great master?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: You are pressed by that two-horned dilemma of which you are so fond of speaking to your disciples.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: For, if you say that by this ‘other’ who does not admit that souls existed before they lived in the body you mean yourself, you have betrayed the secret which in the previous passages was concealed.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: It is now found out that you by your own confession are that other who have fashioned all the doctrines of which you now demand the condemnation.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: But if we are not to believe you to be the ‘other’ of the former passage, so that the doctrines which you now impugn may not be ascribed to you, we have no right to consider you in this case to be the ‘other’ who does not admit that our souls existed before we lived in bodies.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Choose either side you like as the ground of your acquittal.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: This ‘other,’ whom you so frequently bring in, are we to understand by him yourself or some one else?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Do you wish that he should be thought by us to be a catholic or a heretic?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Is he to be acquitted or condemned?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: If that ‘other’ of yours is a catholic, the man who said in the former passage that before this visible world our souls had their abode among the angels and the other heavenly powers in the heavenly places in Jerusalem which is above, and that they there contracted those dispositions which caused the diversities of their birth into the world and of the other conditions to which they are now subject, then these must be esteemed to be catholic doctrines, and we know that it is an impiety to condemn what is catholic.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: But if you call this ‘other’ a heretic, you must also brand as a heretic the ‘other’ who will not admit that souls existed and hoped in Christ before they were born in the body.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Which way can you get out of this dilemma, my master?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Whither will you break forth?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: To what place will you escape?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Whichever way you betake yourself, you will stick fast.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Not only is there no avenue by which you can withdraw yourself; there is not even the least breathing space left you.

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Is this all the profit you have gained from Alexander’s Commentaries on Aristotle, and Porphyry’s Introduction?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Is this the result of the training of all those great Philosophers by whom you tell us you were educated, with all their learning, Greek and Latin, and Jewish into the bargain?

verses.book: The Apology of Rufinus. Addressed to Apronianus, in Reply to Jerome's Letter to Pammachius.
verses.text: Have they ended by bringing you into these inextricable straits, in which you are so pitifully confined that the very Alps could give you no refuge?
